name: structlog
what_it_does: Structured logging library for Python that produces machine-readable key-value or JSON log output, supports async, integrates with stdlib logging, and uses a composable processor pipeline.
use_cases: Emitting structured JSON logs from agent workflows for ingestion into Datadog, Splunk, or ELK with consistent field schemas
Binding per-request or per-agent-run context (agent_id, task_id, user_id) to all log entries in a thread or async context
Integrating structured logging into FastAPI or Django services where stdlib logging is already configured by frameworks
Building audit trails for agent tool calls with structured fields that are queryable in log aggregation systems
Async agent pipelines using asyncio where context variables must be propagated correctly across coroutines
not_for: Simple scripts where human-readable colorized output matters more than machine-parseable structure — use Loguru instead
Teams that want zero-configuration logging out of the box — structlog requires explicit processor pipeline configuration
Applications that need built-in log file rotation — structlog delegates output handling to stdlib logging or custom sinks
best_when: You need production-grade structured logging with consistent JSON output, stdlib compatibility, and async support for agent services going into log aggregation infrastructure.
avoid_when: You want a quick drop-in logger with minimal configuration and human-friendly output for development scripts or small projects.

auth.notes: Local Python library, no auth required

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Processor pipeline errors surface clearly; context binding errors are informative; async context variable issues can produce silent missing-context bugs
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Logging operations are append-only and do not affect application state
known_agent_gotchas: structlog.contextvars.bind_contextvars() uses Python contextvars which do not propagate automatically to new threads — must explicitly copy context for thread pool executors
Processor pipeline order is critical and not validated at configuration time — wrong order silently produces malformed output
Mixing structlog and stdlib logging requires careful setup of stdlib integration or third-party library logs will bypass structlog processors
structlog is not thread-safe by default for shared logger instances — use thread-local or contextvars-based context for concurrent agents
JSON output field ordering is not guaranteed unless using a renderer that sorts keys, which can break downstream schema validation

security_score_components.security_notes: No network layer; secure by default. Bound context variables persist for the duration of the context — ensure secrets are not bound as context vars.
